title: Google Antigravity
description: Agent-first development environment with editor, terminal, browser, and multi-agent workflow surfaces.
controlNotes: Treat as a high-capability local agent: file access, shell execution, browser activity, model usage, and workspace artifacts should be observable.

visualIdentity.label: G
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated brand hint from Google Antigravity official site

observeGuide.summary: Antigravity is expected to behave like an agentic IDE: local workspace reads and writes, terminal commands, browser verification, artifacts, and model calls may all appear as separate activity.

signals.label: Workspace file access
signals.meaning: The agent may read or modify project files in a selected workspace.
signals.detail: Use file/folder observations, IDE workspace paths, and command working directories as the strongest local evidence.
signals.userAction: Review the touched path in Activity, then create an ask-first or block rule for private folders.

signals.label: Terminal or program execution
signals.meaning: The agent may run commands, tests, package managers, or helper programs.
signals.detail: Process launch, terminal tool, and local shell evidence should be separated from ordinary file reads.
signals.userAction: Use ask-first for commands, and also tighten Antigravity's own command approval settings.

signals.label: Browser verification
signals.meaning: The agent may open a browser, inspect pages, or create visual verification artifacts.
signals.detail: Without a browser extension, Pollek can usually see domains and app/window metadata, not raw HTTPS page bodies.
signals.userAction: Install or enable browser observation only if you need page-level details.

observeGuide.caveat: Reference intel explains expected Antigravity behavior. Only local observed events prove what happened on this device.

title: Google Gemini
category: Multimodal model and AI assistant family
description: Gemini is Google's multimodal AI model and assistant family, used through Gemini apps, APIs, AI Studio, coding agents, and tool integrations.
controlNotes: Separate model/API usage, browser or app sessions, file inputs, long-context documents, function/tool calls, and code execution signals.

visualIdentity.label: G
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated model hint from Google Gemini API documentation

observeGuide.summary: Gemini should be shown as a model/provider layer when another agent, such as Antigravity, uses it. The user needs to see both the local agent and the Gemini model/API activity.

signals.label: Model/provider activity
signals.meaning: A local AI agent may be using Gemini as the reasoning/model backend.
signals.detail: Attribute local file and command activity to the local agent, while showing Gemini as the model/provider when API evidence exists.
signals.userAction: Review both the agent row and model/provider row before creating spend or data-sharing rules.

signals.label: Tool or code execution capability
signals.meaning: Gemini API workflows may include tool use, code execution, URL context, Google Search, or computer-use style actions.
signals.detail: Definitions can explain these capabilities; exact calls require API, wrapper, or tool telemetry.
signals.userAction: Route tools through a wrapper/proxy when exact tool-call history is required.

signals.label: File and long-context inputs
signals.meaning: Gemini-capable apps may process documents, media, or long-context data.
signals.detail: Pollek should show file/path metadata and avoid storing raw file contents in the dashboard timeline.
signals.userAction: Protect sensitive folders and keep file content capture disabled unless the user explicitly opts in.

observeGuide.caveat: Reference intel explains Gemini capabilities. It does not prove which prompt, file, or model request occurred locally.

title: ChatGPT
description: General AI assistant surface that can involve browser sessions, uploaded files, connectors, and model usage.
controlNotes: Browser and desktop observation should separate prompt activity, file uploads, cloud-drive connectors, and exact token usage when wrapper or proxy telemetry is available.

visualIdentity.label: AI
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated brand hint from ChatGPT official app

observeGuide.summary: ChatGPT can appear as a browser session, desktop app, app/connector access, file attachment flow, or model/API usage depending on how the user runs it.

signals.label: Browser or desktop session
signals.meaning: The AI activity may be happening in a web tab, browser app, or ChatGPT desktop process.
signals.detail: Process/window evidence can identify the surface; browser-extension or approved integration is needed for richer page/session details.
signals.userAction: Use Activity to confirm the surface, then configure ChatGPT app permissions or browser observation as needed.

signals.label: Files and connected apps
signals.meaning: ChatGPT may reference uploaded files or connected app data depending on user-granted permissions.
signals.detail: Pollek should distinguish local file paths, cloud app hosts, OAuth/connectors, and write actions because they carry different risk.
signals.userAction: Review Settings > Apps inside ChatGPT for read/write permission, and use Pollek rules for local folders.

signals.label: Model usage and cost
signals.meaning: Usage can be exact only when an approved local integration sees provider usage fields.
signals.detail: Without SDK/proxy/wrapper telemetry, Pollek should label usage as estimated or metadata-only.
signals.userAction: Enable a wrapper/proxy for exact usage, or keep estimates visible but clearly labeled.

observeGuide.caveat: Reference intel does not mean ChatGPT accessed a file or app. Local activity events and ChatGPT's own permissions remain the proof.

title: OpenAI Codex
description: Coding agent surface that can write code, run tasks, manage projects, and operate through desktop or CLI workflows.
controlNotes: Classify workspace file access, command execution, package manager activity, Git operations, browser/test verification, and model usage separately.

visualIdentity.label: CX
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated brand hint from OpenAI Codex download page

observeGuide.summary: Codex should be explained as a coding agent: the user cares which workspace files were read or changed, what commands ran, whether Git/network/package tools were used, and what model usage was captured.

signals.label: Code and workspace access
signals.meaning: Codex may read, edit, or create files inside the active project workspace.
signals.detail: File paths, diff/write events, and working-directory evidence should be shown as timeline items, not hidden in raw logs.
signals.userAction: Set ask-first for writes outside approved project folders.

signals.label: Command, test, and package activity
signals.meaning: Codex may run tests, build tools, package managers, or helper scripts.
signals.detail: Treat command execution as a separate activity category because it can indirectly read or modify many files.
signals.userAction: Keep command execution on ask-first if the repository or machine contains sensitive data.

signals.label: Git and publishing workflow
signals.meaning: Codex may inspect diffs, create commits, or push changes when allowed by the user.
signals.detail: Git events should be grouped with file writes and network activity so the user understands what left the machine.
signals.userAction: Review Git actions in History before allowing pushes from an agent.

observeGuide.caveat: Reference intel describes expected Codex workflows. Pollek still needs local observation, shell wrapper logs, or OS audit sources to prove exact actions.

title: Claude
category: AI assistant and coding agent family
description: Claude includes web, desktop, coding, cowork, and extension surfaces depending on deployment.
controlNotes: Classify browser sessions, desktop app processes, Claude Code shells, MCP tools, and cloud connector activity separately.

visualIdentity.label: C
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated brand hint from Anthropic Claude product overview

observeGuide.summary: Claude may appear as a web app, desktop app, Claude Code CLI, Chrome extension, connector, or API/model provider. Observation should separate each surface.

signals.label: Desktop, web, or Chrome surface
signals.meaning: The same Claude account can be used through several local surfaces with different observable evidence.
signals.detail: Window/process metadata identifies the surface; exact prompts, page content, or connector records need approved integrations.
signals.userAction: Check Claude's own connected apps and permissions when Pollek reports browser or connector activity.

signals.label: Claude Code or command workflow
signals.meaning: Claude Code can operate like a coding agent that reads files and runs commands.
signals.detail: Treat shell execution, workspace file access, and model usage as separate timeline categories.
signals.userAction: Use ask-first for terminal commands and protect folders outside the active project.

signals.label: MCP tools and connectors
signals.meaning: Claude integrations may expose external tools, files, or cloud data through MCP or connector surfaces.
signals.detail: MCP config paths and tool schemas are definition evidence; actual tool calls need wrapper/proxy/telemetry events.
signals.userAction: Route MCP servers through a Pollek wrapper or proxy when you need exact tool-call history.

observeGuide.caveat: Reference intel gives expected Claude surfaces. It is not a claim that Claude accessed a file, email, or connector on this device.

title: DeepSeek
description: Model provider commonly observed through API calls, browser sessions, SDKs, and local agent wrappers.
controlNotes: Exact usage should prefer provider response usage, proxy logs, SDK logs, or wrapper telemetry before falling back to estimates.

visualIdentity.label: DS
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated brand hint from DeepSeek API documentation

observeGuide.summary: DeepSeek is most often observed as model/API network egress, SDK usage, an agent backend, or a browser/chat session rather than as a local desktop agent.

signals.label: API endpoint use
signals.meaning: A local app or agent may be sending model requests to DeepSeek-compatible API endpoints.
signals.detail: Network metadata can identify the host. Exact prompt, response, token, or cost fields require SDK/proxy/wrapper telemetry.
signals.userAction: Keep DeepSeek API calls labeled as model usage and enable proxy telemetry for exact cost.

signals.label: Agent backend model
signals.meaning: Another AI agent may use DeepSeek as the model provider behind its own UI.
signals.detail: Pollek should attribute the local agent and the model provider separately when both are visible.
signals.userAction: Review both the AI app and model provider rows in Activity before creating a rule.

signals.label: Usage fields
signals.meaning: DeepSeek-compatible responses may expose usage details through approved API integrations.
signals.detail: If only domain/process metadata is available, Pollek must show estimated usage separately from exact usage.
signals.userAction: Treat estimated usage as directional until exact SDK/proxy logs are available.

observeGuide.caveat: Reference intel classifies DeepSeek surfaces. Local evidence is still required to prove which app called the API.

title: Manus
description: Autonomous agent surface focused on completing multi-step online tasks and generating artifacts.
controlNotes: Classify as a high-autonomy agent when observed: browser actions, file generation, cloud task execution, and user approvals should be separated in evidence.

visualIdentity.label: M
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated brand hint from Manus official site

observeGuide.summary: Manus should be explained as a high-autonomy agent surface: web tasks, browser operation, generated files, desktop/mobile apps, integrations, and cloud task execution may all appear.

signals.label: Browser and web automation
signals.meaning: Manus may operate web pages or cloud tasks through a browser-like surface.
signals.detail: Domain/window metadata can prove surface activity; page-level action details require approved browser or app integration.
signals.userAction: Use warning or ask-first for risky web actions and review Manus permissions inside Manus.

signals.label: Generated files and downloads
signals.meaning: Manus may create deliverables, slides, websites, apps, or downloaded artifacts.
signals.detail: File write events should be shown as timeline entries with target path and action result.
signals.userAction: Protect Downloads, Desktop, and project folders if Manus is allowed to generate files.

signals.label: Connected services
signals.meaning: Manus may interact with apps such as mail, Slack, API, or team integrations when configured.
signals.detail: Pollek can classify known hosts and integrations from definition data, but exact actions require integration logs.
signals.userAction: Check Manus integration settings and use Pollek Activity to confirm which service was touched.

observeGuide.caveat: Reference intel makes Manus understandable to users. It does not replace local observation or Manus's own permission records.

title: Google Drive
description: Cloud file storage and collaboration surface often accessed by AI agents through browser sessions, connectors, OAuth scopes, or drive APIs.
controlNotes: Use observed host, OAuth scopes, file names, and connector IDs as local evidence. Reference intel helps classify the resource; it does not prove access.

visualIdentity.label: D
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated brand hint from Google Drive official site

title: Anthropic API
description: Provider API endpoint for Claude models and agent integrations.
controlNotes: Exact token and cost records should come from provider response usage, proxy telemetry, SDK logs, or wrapper events before estimates.

visualIdentity.label: A
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated brand hint from Anthropic developer docs

title: OpenAI API
description: Provider API endpoint for OpenAI model, tool, and agent workloads.
controlNotes: Prefer exact usage from API responses, SDK logs, proxy wrappers, or MCP wrapper events.

visualIdentity.label: OAI
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated brand hint from OpenAI platform documentation

title: Model Context Protocol
description: Protocol surface for exposing tools, resources, and prompts to AI clients.
controlNotes: Local enforcement should prioritize MCP proxy or wrapper decision points, parameter visibility, audit logging, and output guard checks.

visualIdentity.label: MCP
visualIdentity.sourceLabel: Pollek curated protocol icon hint from MCP documentation

observeGuide.summary: MCP definitions explain which tools, resources, and prompts an AI client may call. Pollek should compare discovered MCP config and schemas with actual tool-call telemetry.

signals.label: Tool definitions
signals.meaning: The MCP server exposes callable tools and parameter schemas to AI clients.
signals.detail: A discovered definition is capability evidence. A timeline tool-call event is action evidence.
signals.userAction: Use MCP wrapper/proxy mode when users need ask-first or blocking before tools run.

signals.label: Resource visibility
signals.meaning: The MCP server may expose resource links, files, records, or templates.
signals.detail: Some resources may appear only in tool results, so Pollek should inspect approved tool telemetry as well as resource lists.
signals.userAction: Show resource links in Data & Apps and let users create folder/domain/resource rules.

signals.label: Prompt and output guard point
signals.meaning: MCP wrapper or proxy is a strong place to observe prompt/tool arguments and apply safety guards.
signals.detail: Raw prompt capture should remain opt-in; default dashboard views should show redacted metadata and decisions.
signals.userAction: Enable Prompt Guard only for the agent paths where the user wants safety checks.

observeGuide.caveat: MCP reference intel explains protocol capability. Actual tool use is proven only by wrapper/proxy/telemetry events.
